Site information

title: How to write a page using LaTeX
text:
In the scientific community, it is very common to have texts, equations, and full documents already written in LaTeX. Instead of manually rewriting these documents using Quarto Markdown syntax, this template provides an automatic script that replaces the most common LaTeX environments and commands with their Quarto Markdown equivalents. To distinguish these hybrid files from standard LaTeX documents, a custom .qtex extension is used.
This document serves as both a guide and a test file for the script. It is written using the supported LaTeX commands to explain exactly how the transformation works. Because this script is a simple text replacer and not a full LaTeX compiler, any LaTeX environment or command that is not explicitly demonstrated on this page (such as \begin{table} or complex macros) is not supported and will not be automatically replaced. For those unsupported elements, native Quarto Markdown syntax must be used. Additionally, all the standard Quarto Markdown commands explained in the Markdown guide can be used.

title: How to write a page using Markdown
text: GitHub Pages can transform simple plain text files into HTML webpages. For this template the files have .qmd extension and follow the Quarto Markdown syntax. Below are examples of the formatting you will use most often.

title: \(\Lambda\)CDM Cosmological Model
text:
Field equations
In order to describe an homogeneous and isotropic universe like in the framework of GR, one should solve the Einstein field equations using a homogeneous, isotropic, and expanding metric (the FLRW metric), \[
ds^2 = dt^2 - a^2(t) \left(\frac{dr^2}{1-k r^2}+r^2 d\Omega\right) \,,
\tag{1}\] where \(k\) is the curvature and \(a(t)\) the scale factor of the universe, which is closely related to the redshift, \(a(t)=1/(1+z(t))\). We can use this relation to also connect the temporal coordinate to the redshift variable, \[
a(t)=\frac{1}{1+z(t)} \;\to\; \frac{\dot a(t)}{a(t)} dt = -\frac{dz}{1+z(t)} \;\to\; dt = -\frac{dz}{H(z)(1+z)} \,,
\tag{2}\] where we have defined the Hubble parameter as \(H=\dot a/a\).


Friedman equations
Using the FLRW metric, Eq. (1), to solve the Einstein equations leads to the Friedmann equations, which are the basis of the standard cosmology. The first Friedmann equation can be written in terms of the cosmic density of radiation (\(\Omega_r\)), matter (\(\Omega_m\)), curvature (\(\Omega_k\)), and dark energy (\(\Omega_\Lambda\)), \[
H(z) = H_0 \sqrt{\Omega_r (1+z)^4 + \Omega_m (1+z)^3 + \Omega_k (1+z)^2 + \Omega_\Lambda} \,.
\tag{3}\] The model does not predict the values of these cosmic densities, so they should be adjusted using cosmological observations and measurements. For more information see [1]






References

[1] Reyes Hung M A 2023 Exploration of possible signals beyond special relativity using high-energy astroparticle physics PhD thesis (U. Zaragoza (main))
